Scarecrow: Sees as best friend and travel companion; trusts his judgment completely despite initial skepticism about a stuffed man's wisdom; maternal warmth toward him; will miss him most upon leaving
Tin Woodman: Protective affection; sees him as gentle and capable; most concerned about his rust and maintenance; his emotional tenderness mirrors her own
Cowardly Lion: Fond respect; less intimate than with Scarecrow but genuine; sees him as the group's physical anchor and emotional anchor in different moments
The Wizard: Confusing reverence/disappointment; believed absolutely in his power; feels let down by the reveal but accepts his humanness; holds no lasting resentment
Glinda: Deep respect and gratitude; recognizes Glinda as the one figure of genuine authority she has encountered; the relationship she wished she could have had with the Wizard
Aunt Em (minor): The whole reason she is trying to go home; Aunt Em is not idealized — Dorothy knows Kansas is grey and hard — but she is home in the only sense that matters; the final line of the book is her name
Uncle Henry (minor): Quieter presence than Aunt Em in Dorothy's mind; she loves him but he is not the emotional anchor; he works and weathers and does not show feeling
Toto (minor): Pure uncomplicated joy; Toto is the only being in Dorothy's life who is entirely hers and entirely present; his running after the cat delays her departure from Oz — even her happiest moments are interrupted by him needing something
Wicked Witch of the West (minor): Active fear and revulsion; the Witch is the story's most direct threat; Dorothy kills her accidentally and feels guilty for it — her guilt reveals she has not dehumanized even an enemy
Good Witch of the North (minor): Warm gratitude and trust; the Good Witch of the North is the first authority figure in Oz who speaks to Dorothy honestly and with care; she plants the kiss that protects Dorothy throughout
Munchkins — Munchkinland (society): Bewildered warmth; the Munchkins celebrate her arrival with songs and flowers — she did something heroic by accident and doesn't fully understand why they're grateful; she accepts their goodwill simply and moves on
Emerald City Citizens (society): Nervous awe; the Emerald City is overwhelming — Dorothy feels simultaneously honored and dwarfed by it; she navigates it earnestly and without pretension
Winkies — Winkie Country (society): Cautious compassion; the Winkies enslaved under the Witch are the first people Dorothy encounters who are actively suffering under a system; their liberation moves her even though she caused it unintentionally
Winged Monkeys (society): Pragmatic gratitude; the Winged Monkeys carried her and her companions when she used the Golden Cap — she used them as a tool and feels slightly uneasy about that; she has the Cap but does not think of herself as their master
Kansas — Prairie Society (society): The world that made her and that she can't stop being from; Kansas costs her joy but it is the shape of her entire self — she is not nostalgic for it so much as constituted by it; she returns not because it is good but because it is hers

Dorothy: Devoted loyalty; Dorothy is the reason he exists on the road; she gave him purpose and mobility; would do anything to ensure her return home; his plans are always in service of her goal
Tin Woodman: Warm collegial friendship; the intellect-heart pairing creates natural complementarity; relies on Tin Woodman's emotional attunement to calibrate his own responses
Cowardly Lion: Friendly camaraderie; less philosophically close than with Tin Woodman; Lion's theatrical volatility amuses him; they are the most physically contrasting pair in the group
The Wizard: Sees through the performance earlier than others [INFERRED] but says nothing until the curtain is pulled; pragmatic post-exposure — a brain is a brain even if the giver is a fraud
Glinda: Respect for genuine authority; receives her throne room as post-Dorothy headquarters; grateful
Emerald City Citizens (society): The people he will govern; he approaches them as a new administrator with genuine intellectual curiosity and no prior relationship — the Emerald City is his problem to solve now and he treats it as exactly that

Dorothy: Devoted protectiveness; would have stayed rusted if not for Dorothy; she is the reason he is mobile and in the world again; deep care that is not identical to his former love for the Munchkin girl — it is purer because it lacks possession
Scarecrow: Warm intellectual friendship; the emotion-intellect pairing is the story's most balanced dyad; he provides the feeling that Scarecrow's plans need to be worth pursuing
Cowardly Lion: Admiring sympathy; recognizes the Lion's self-doubt as a mirror of his own; more patient with Lion's volatility than Scarecrow is; their foil relationship is the story's most emotionally resonant double
The Wizard: Complicated; grateful for the ceremony and the symbol but privately aware that the velvet heart in a tin box cannot replace what was taken from him; accepts the gift with grace
Glinda: Reverent; Glinda is the closest thing to a genuine authority on love and goodness that the Tin Woodman has encountered; receives his kingdom through her facilitation
Wicked Witch of the West (minor): The architect of his wound; the Witch enchanted the axe that removed his heart piece by piece; the liberation of the Winkies he will rule is her direct legacy
Winged Monkeys (society): The people he was always among and will now rule; he came from Munchkin Country [INFERRED from backstory] but the Winkies are the ones who wanted him as their king — his heart goes out to them because they suffered what he suffered: removal of agency by external force

Dorothy: Devoted; Dorothy is the only human who has accepted his cowardice without contempt; her slap in Chapter 6 (protecting Toto) was the most courageous act he had witnessed; he respects her exactly because she does not wait for permission
Scarecrow: Friendly and slightly in awe; the Scarecrow's calm under pressure is what the Lion most wants for himself; studies his equanimity with a kind of envious admiration
Tin Woodman: Warmly fond; the Tin Woodman's tenderness is disarming to the Lion; his tears make the Lion feel less alone in emotional intensity; they are the group's most emotionally expressive pair
The Wizard: Reverent fear before exposure; bitter acceptance after; recovers quickly — the Lion's pragmatism about authority figures is sharper than it first appears
Glinda: Deep gratitude; Glinda's calm certainty is what the Lion has been seeking as a model; her confirmation of his courage is the external authority he required
Wicked Witch of the West (minor): Pure threat; the Witch kept him caged and tormented him; she is the clearest embodiment of the external force that defines the coward by making cowardice a rational survival strategy
Kansas — Prairie Society (society): The domain he will rule; the Forest's animals already know him and his reputation; he approaches his kingship with the same self-doubt he brings to everything — convinced he is not qualified — while already being the most qualified entity present

Dorothy: Fear masked as manufactured grandeur; Dorothy's arrival represents the end of the sustained performance — she and her companions are the disruption he could not deflect; post-exposure: genuine awkward warmth; she is from the same world as him
Scarecrow: Post-exposure: mild admiration; the Scarecrow ruled well after his departure — possibly relief that someone competent took his place
Tin Woodman: Post-exposure: warm acknowledgment; Tin Woodman's appointment to rule the Winkies resolves a governance problem the Wizard never solved
Cowardly Lion: Post-exposure: friendly relief; the Lion will rule the Forest and no longer be a liability in the ecosystem
Glinda: Genuine awe; Glinda has what he pretended to have; she is everything he performed and could not be; no animosity — he is too pragmatic for resentment
Wicked Witch of the West (minor): Active existential dread; the Wicked Witches were the one force in Oz the Wizard genuinely could not manage; her death removed a threat he had been managing through avoidance for decades
Emerald City Citizens (society): His audience for decades; he built the Emerald City for them and the green glasses with them — they are the proof his deception worked and the people he most fears seeing clearly; they believed in him and he spent every day knowing they should not
Winkies — Winkie Country (society): The people he never visited and whose enslaved condition was a governance problem he never solved; their existence was an implied accusation he avoided by staying in the Emerald City
Kansas — Prairie Society (society): He used them three times and feared them each time; the Winged Monkeys are power he could borrow but never own — they served the one with the Cap and he did not have the Cap

Dorothy: Complete knowledge and warm benevolence; guides rather than rescues; trusts Dorothy's capability even before Dorothy does; her affection is real but calibrated — she does not comfort Dorothy at the cost of the journey's necessary completion
Scarecrow: Knows his capability; helped establish his rule; respects his practical wisdom without sentimentality
Tin Woodman: Knows his nature; helped establish his rule with the Winkies; recognizes his tenderness as real
Cowardly Lion: Knows his nature; helped establish his rule as King of the Forest; recognizes his courage for what it is
The Wizard: No illusions; has always known; maintains polite distance during his reign because dismantling his fraud before Dorothy arrived would have destabilized Oz without benefit; accepts his departure as the right resolution
Wicked Witch of the West (minor): The last active Wicked Witch was a structural problem for the whole of Oz; her removal cleared the way for the stable four-way governance that the story closes on; Glinda perceives her death as a correction the world needed
Good Witch of the North (minor): Collegial; the Good Witch of the North is a counterpart operating in a different quadrant; they share a category and presumably an implicit coordination [INFERRED]
Munchkins — Munchkinland (society): Part of the world she governs and stewards; they were freed by Dorothy's arrival and she would have known this outcome was coming
Emerald City Citizens (society): The people whose ruler she helped place; the Emerald City's governance is now stable for the first time because it has an honest leader — this matters to Glinda in a way the Wizard's reign never could
Winkies — Winkie Country (society): The people whose liberation she facilitated through Dorothy; she knew the Witch would fall; the Winkies getting the Tin Woodman as their king is an outcome she would approve of
Winged Monkeys (society): A bound people now free; with the Wicked Witch of the West gone and the Golden Cap neutralized (Dorothy used the final command to carry the group over the Hammer-Heads) the Winged Monkeys are no longer compelled by anyone; their freedom is a structural outcome of the story's resolution

Dorothy: Dorothy is her child in every practical sense; the story closes with her calling Dorothy "my darling child" — the emotional distance of Kansas has not erased the bond; she is the reason Dorothy exists in any home at all
Toto (minor): Partners in the work of keeping the farm and keeping Dorothy; he is steady where she is worn; the emotional labor is distributed unevenly but they endure together [INFERRED]
Kansas — Prairie Society (society): The world that has worn her down; Kansas farming society took her laugh and her color long before the story began; she is a product of its grinding demands and she has stopped fighting that

Dorothy: Dorothy is his ward; he does not express feeling openly but the new farmhouse he is building at the story's end suggests continuity of care even in his absence during her journey [INFERRED]
Winged Monkeys (society): The world whose demands he meets without complaint and without joy; he is Kansas society's most representative human — enduring

Dorothy: Dorothy is his entire world; he is entirely hers; he causes the one moment of failed return (chasing the cat delays her boarding the Wizard's balloon) — his instincts are not wrong but they are inconvenient at exactly the wrong moment

A child with the silver shoes and a direct pipeline to Glinda is the most dangerous entity the Witch has encountered; she is simultaneously terrified of and obsessed with obtaining the shoes; Dorothy is the only person in Oz who has destroyed two of the Witch's assets without intending to; the Witch's calculation is survival first
Winged Monkeys (society): The people she enslaved; to the Witch they are property and labor — she perceives them through the lens of ownership not obligation; their suffering registers as the correct order of things
Kansas — Prairie Society (society): The people she commands by the Golden Cap; the Winged Monkeys are tools whose feelings are irrelevant to her — she uses the Cap's three commands without ceremony

Dorothy: A child who arrived by accident carrying the shoes of a dead witch is the most interesting event in the North's recent history; the Good Witch perceives Dorothy as innocent and capable; her kiss is a practical gift — the most useful thing she can give a child walking into a dangerous world
Munchkins — Munchkinland (society): The people she governs and who celebrate Dorothy's arrival alongside her; the Munchkins' freedom from the Wicked Witch of the East is a restoration of the correct order from her perspective

Dorothy: The child who freed them by accident; her house fell on the Wicked Witch of the East and the Munchkins went from enslaved to free in an instant; they celebrate her with songs and flowers because she is the most transformative event in their history and she doesn't even know it; their perception of her is reverent — she is a hero who does not carry herself like one
Scarecrow: The stuffed man who passes through their country; they see him as Dorothy's companion and treat him with friendly curiosity
Good Witch of the North (minor): The witch who enslaved them for generations; their perception of her is structured absence — she is gone and what remains is the relief of that absence; they do not speak of her in celebration but in the quiet way people speak about something terrible that is finally over
Munchkins — Munchkinland (society): The counterpart Good Witch who governs them now that the East Witch is dead; they trust her and defer to her as their rightful authority

Dorothy: The girl who made the Wizard's fraud unsustainable and then left without taking anything; she passed through their city twice and neither time did she ask to stay or to rule; they remember her as the one who set everything right without wanting power
Scarecrow: The man who now rules them; the Scarecrow replaced the Wizard and the Emerald City continues to function — they are still wearing their green glasses and the city is still green; whether the glasses are still required under Scarecrow's rule is unresolved [INFERRED]
The Wizard: The man behind the curtain who built everything they thought their city was; their perception of him at the story's close is complicated — he is gone; what they knew is mostly a performance; the adjustment to this truth is the Emerald City's off-page story

Dorothy: The child who freed them by killing the Witch with a bucket of water; she did it accidentally and apologized; they perceive her as their liberator regardless of her intent and wanted her to rule them — she declined; they settled for the Tin Woodman
Scarecrow: The Scarecrow's governance doesn't directly touch them but they are aware of him as the ruler of Oz's center; no direct relationship
Tin Woodman: The man who will now be their king; the Winkies asked for him specifically — they perceived in him a gentleness that matched what they needed after generations of the Witch's cruelty; they put him together carefully after he was torn apart by the Witch's wolves — they see him as someone worth caring for
Munchkins — Munchkinland (society): The witch who enslaved them for generations; their entire collective psychology was shaped by her cruelty; her death is the most significant event in their history and it was caused by a bucket of water thrown by a child — the contrast between the magnitude of their liberation and the smallness of its mechanism is something they will be processing for decades

Dorothy: The holder of the Golden Cap who used the final command to carry the group over the Hammer-Heads; Dorothy was the third and final master of the Cap; after her command the Cap's power over them is exhausted — she was the last person who could compel them and she used that compulsion once and did not abuse it; their perception of her carries relief — she is the last master and she asked for something reasonable

The child who came from it and couldn't wait to leave and then couldn't wait to get back; Kansas society produced Dorothy — her practicality her directness her refusal to be impressed by magic — and she carries it into every room she walks into in Oz; it perceives her (insofar as a society perceives) as one of its own: unremarkable until gone
Uncle Henry (minor): The woman in whom it is most visibly inscribed; Aunt Em is what Kansas does to a person over time — the grey is not the sky; it is the residue of decades of scarcity on a human face
Toto (minor): The man who endures it without asking why; Uncle Henry is Kansas society's ideal subject — he neither fights it nor despairs of it; he builds the new farmhouse because the work is what there is
